channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: AEGIS: Adversarial Entropy-Guided Immune System -- Thermodynamic State Space Models for Zero-Day Network Evasion Detection
item.description: TLS 1.3 encryption has rendered traditional DPI ineffective, prompting reliance on Euclidean Transformer models (e.g., ET-BERT) for encrypted traffic analysis—yet these remain highly vulnerable to byte-level adversarial morphing (e.g., pre-padding attacks drop accuracy to 25.68%; VLESS Reality evades certificate checks entirely). AEGIS introduces a paradigm shift: it abandons payload inspection and instead models network flows as continuous-time thermodynamic systems in a non-Euclidean Poincaré manifold. Leveraging a Thermodynamic Variance-Guided Hyperbolic Liquid State Space Model (TVD-HL-SSM), it extracts six physics-informed features—including microsecond-scale inter-arrival time (IAT) decay via Liquid Time-Constants—and applies a Thermodynamic Variance Detector to expose low-entropy anomalies in automated C2 tunnels. Implemented as a zero-copy C++ eBPF Harvester with an O(N) Mamba-3 core, AEGIS achieves **0.9952 F1-score**, **99.50% TPR**, and **262 µs latency** on an RTX 4090 across a 400GB adversarial corpus spanning backbone, IoT botnets, zero-days, and proprietary VLESS Reality tunnels—setting a new state-of-the-art for physics-grounded zero-day evasion detection.

item.title: APEX: Agent Payment Execution with Policy for Autonomous Agent API Access
item.description: APEX is a production-ready research system that adapts HTTP 402–style payment gating to real-time fiat infrastructure (e.g., UPI), enabling policy-governed, tokenized, and replay-resistant API access for autonomous agents. It implements a challenge-settle-consume lifecycle using HMAC-signed short-lived tokens, idempotent settlement, and policy-aware approval—all built with FastAPI, SQLite, and standard Python libraries for transparency and reproducibility. Evaluated across six scenarios (N=20–40 per scenario), APEX reduces total spending by **27.3%** via policy enforcement while sustaining a **52.8% success rate** for legitimate requests. Its security mechanisms achieve **100% block rate** against replay and invalid-token attacks with only **19.6 ms average latency**, and results show low variance across trials (95% CI). APEX provides the first reference architecture for agent-payment monetization in regulated fiat ecosystems—without compromising security or policy guarantees.

item.title: RuleForge: Automated Generation and Validation for Web Vulnerability Detection at Scale
item.description: RuleForge is an AWS-internal system that automates the generation and validation of JSON-based detection rules for web vulnerabilities from structured Nuclei templates. To address the scalability gap—48,000+ CVEs disclosed in 2025 alone—it leverages LLMs not just for rule synthesis, but critically as a *judge*: a novel confidence validation framework evaluates candidates along sensitivity (minimizing false negatives) and specificity (minimizing false positives), achieving AUROC = 0.75 and reducing production false positives by 67% versus synthetic-only validation. Its 5×5 generation strategy (5 parallel candidates, up to 5 refinement rounds each) and systematic human-in-the-loop feedback integration enable continuous quality improvement. Extensions demonstrate rule generation from unstructured sources (e.g., CVE descriptions, PoC code) and a proof-of-concept agentic workflow for cross-event detection. Key lessons underscore the necessity of domain-guided prompt engineering and expert-led validation to mitigate LLM overconfidence and ensure operational reliability.

item.title: Contextualizing Sink Knowledge for Java Vulnerability Discovery
item.description: Java applications frequently suffer from vulnerabilities due to insecure usage of security-sensitive APIs (e.g., file I/O enabling path traversal, deserialization enabling RCE). These “sink” APIs encode two critical vulnerability-specific properties: (1) *reachability constraints* (program-specific data/control-flow conditions required to invoke the sink), and (2) *exploitability conditions* (semantic requirements to trigger the flaw). Existing fuzzers like Jazzer largely ignore such sink semantics, limiting their effectiveness. We present **GONDAR**, a sink-centric Java fuzzing framework that systematically leverages sink knowledge via CWE-guided scanning, LLM-assisted static filtering, and two collaborative agents: an *exploration agent* that solves path constraints to reach sinks, and an *exploitation agent* that synthesizes PoC exploits by satisfying vulnerability-triggering conditions. GONDAR tightly integrates these agents with a coverage-guided fuzzer through continuous seed and runtime feedback exchange. Evaluated on real-world Java benchmarks, GONDAR discovers **4× more vulnerabilities than Jazzer**, demonstrated strong performance in the DARPA AI Cyber Challenge, and is deployed in OpenSSF’s OSS-CRS sandbox for open-source security hardening.

item.title: Seclens: Role-specific Evaluation of LLM's for security vulnerablity detection
item.description: Existing LLM vulnerability detection benchmarks rely on single-aggregate metrics, obscuring critical stakeholder-specific trade-offs—e.g., CISOs prioritize recall of critical flaws, engineering leads demand low false positives, and AI officers balance capability against cost and latency. To address this, we introduce **SecLens-R**, a multi-stakeholder evaluation framework with 35 shared dimensions grouped into 7 categories (e.g., localization precision, fix quality, inference cost). It defines five role-specific weighting profiles (CISO, Chief AI Officer, Security Researcher, Head of Engineering, AI-as-Actor), each selecting 12–16 dimensions with weights summing to 80 to compute a 0–100 Decision Score. Evaluated on 406 real-world vulnerability tasks across 10 languages and 8 OWASP categories (using 12 frontier models in both Code-in-Prompt and Tool-Use settings), SecLens-R reveals dramatic score variance—up to **31 points for the same model across roles** (e.g., Qwen3-Coder: 76.3 under Engineering vs. 45.2 under CISO; GPT-5.4 shows analogous divergence). These results establish vulnerability detection as inherently multi-objective and demonstrate that stakeholder-aware evaluation uncovers actionable insights obscured by monolithic metrics.

item.title: AI-Assisted Hardware Security Verification: A Survey and AI Accelerator Case Study
item.description: This survey presents a structured analysis of AI-assisted hardware security verification, organizing recent advances across six workflow stages: asset identification, threat modeling, security test-plan generation, simulation-driven analysis, formal verification, and countermeasure reasoning. We demonstrate practical applicability through a case study on the open-source NVIDIA Deep Learning Accelerator (NVDLA), showing how LLMs and ML models accelerate threat tree generation, UVM testbench synthesis for fault injection and privilege escalation, and assertion refinement for formal security properties. Key findings indicate up to 60% reduction in threat-modeling time and 5× faster test-plan creation—yet all AI outputs must be grounded in simulation traces, formal proofs, and benchmark-driven evaluation (e.g., CHES-CTF, RISC-V Security WG suites) to ensure trustworthiness. The work establishes AI not as a replacement, but as a rigorous, evidence-anchored augmentation layer in hardware security assurance.

item.title: SEAL: An Open, Auditable, and Fair Data Generation Framework for AI-Native 6G Networks
item.description: SEAL is the first open, auditable, and fairness-aware synthetic data generation framework tailored for AI-native 6G networks. Addressing critical data scarcity and regulatory compliance gaps, SEAL introduces two novel components: (1) an *Ethical and Regulatory Compliance by Design (ERCD)* module that embeds real-time bias detection, multi-fairness auditing (e.g., equalized odds), and ISO/IEC 23894–aligned audit trails; and (2) a *Federated Learning feedback loop* that privately calibrates synthetic data using aggregated insights from live 6G testbeds across five countries. Evaluated on 3GPP R18 channel modeling, URLLC slicing, and mmWave beam prediction, SEAL achieves a 42.7% lower Frechet Inception Distance, 0.018 equalized odds disparity (vs. 0.132 baseline), and 9.3–14.1% higher task accuracy than state-of-the-art GAN- and diffusion-based methods. All code, logs, and datasets are publicly released under Apache 2.0.

item.title: Development and multi-center evaluation of domain-adapted speech recognition for human-AI teaming in real-world gastrointestinal endoscopy
item.description: EndoASR is a domain-adapted automatic speech recognition (ASR) system designed for real-time, reliable human-AI teaming in gastrointestinal endoscopy. To overcome limitations of general-purpose ASR—especially poor handling of medical terminology and noisy clinical acoustics—we propose a two-stage adaptation strategy: (1) language modeling fine-tuning on synthetic endoscopy reports covering anatomy, pathology, and procedures; and (2) acoustic model robustness enhancement using real-world noise-augmented data. Evaluated retrospectively across six endoscopists, EndoASR reduced character error rate (CER) from 20.52% to 14.14% and improved medical term accuracy (Med ACC) from 54.30% to 87.59%. In a prospective multi-center study across five independent endoscopy centers, it consistently outperformed the baseline Paraformer model (CER: 16.20% → 14.97%; Med ACC: 61.63% → 84.16%). With only 220M parameters and an ultra-low real-time factor (RTF = 0.005), EndoASR enables efficient edge deployment and significantly boosts downstream LLM-based structured information extraction and clinician-AI interaction.

item.title: GPA: Learning GUI Process Automation from Demonstrations
item.description: **GPA: Learning GUI Process Automation from Demonstrations** introduces a lightweight, vision-based Robotic Process Automation (RPA) framework that achieves deterministic, robust, and private GUI automation from a *single demonstration*. To overcome the fragility of script-based RPA and the non-determinism of VLM-driven GUI agents, GPA integrates three key innovations: (1) **Sequential Monte Carlo localization**, explicitly modeling scaling, occlusion, and detection uncertainty for pixel-accurate element tracking across resolutions; (2) **Readiness calibration**, enforcing strict pre-execution validation of element visibility, interactivity, and contextual consistency to guarantee deterministic behavior; and (3) **Fully local, zero-data-leak execution**, enabling sub-200ms/step inference on commodity hardware. Evaluated on long-horizon GUI tasks (avg. 12 steps), GPA achieves **94.1% success rate—27.3 percentage points higher than Gemini 3 Pro with CUA—and runs 10.2× faster (2.1s vs. 21.5s avg. task time)**. As a composable MCP/CLI tool, GPA enables LLM agents to delegate GUI execution while retaining high-level reasoning—bridging the gap between symbolic planning and pixel-perfect action.

item.title: A Role-Based LLM Framework for Structured Information Extraction from Healthy Food Policies
item.description: This paper introduces a **role-based LLM framework** to address hallucination, misclassification, and omission issues in structured information extraction (IE) from heterogeneous healthy food policies. Unlike generic prompting methods, it decomposes expert reasoning into three specialized roles—*Policy Analyst*, *Legal Strategy Specialist*, and *Food System Expert*—each guided by explicit, domain-structured knowledge (e.g., legal mechanism definitions and food system stage criteria) embedded in role-specific prompts. Evaluated on 608 real-world policies from the Healthy Food Policy Project (HFPP) using Llama-3.3-70B, our framework achieves **+22.7% average F1 gain** in legal mechanism identification and **+18.3%** in food system stage classification over zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought baselines. It delivers not only superior accuracy in complex, multi-step reasoning but also **transparent, auditable, and knowledge-grounded outputs**, establishing a new standard for trustworthy policy IE.

item.title: BVFLMSP : Bayesian Vertical Federated Learning for Multimodal Survival with Privacy
item.description: Multimodal time-to-event prediction faces dual challenges: strict privacy constraints prevent centralized data aggregation, while existing models lack uncertainty quantification—limiting clinical trust. We propose **BVFLMSP**, a Bayesian vertical federated learning framework for privacy-preserving multimodal survival analysis. It employs split neural networks where each client models one modality via a Bayesian neural network, and the server aggregates perturbed (Gaussian DP) intermediate representations to perform Bayesian Cox-based risk prediction. Evaluated on UK Biobank and TCGA, BVFLMSP achieves up to **+0.021 C-index** over MultiSurv (centralized multimodal baseline) and outperforms unimodal baselines significantly. Under varying privacy budgets ($\varepsilon = 1.0$–$4.0$), it maintains robust performance (C-index drop ≤0.009) while delivering calibrated uncertainty estimates—enabling reliable, interpretable, and provably private survival modeling.

item.title: Demographic Parity Tails for Regression
item.description: We propose **DP-Tails**, a novel framework for fairness-aware regression that enforces demographic parity *only in the tails* of the target distribution—e.g., high-risk or extreme-value regions—rather than globally. Motivated by real-world applications where fairness concerns are inherently localized, our approach leverages optimal transport theory to define a geometrically grounded, differentiable tail-alignment loss based on truncated Wasserstein distance. We design an interpretable, plug-and-play algorithm compatible with any regressor (e.g., neural networks or gradient-boosted trees), supported by theoretical guarantees: (i) finite-sample risk bounds showing controlled accuracy degradation; (ii) formal fairness properties for tail-specific independence. Experiments on ACS Income, Diabetes, and Law School datasets demonstrate that DP-Tails improves tail fairness (e.g., 42.7% reduction in 90th-percentile group disparity) while increasing mean absolute error by only 1.3%—a substantially better fairness–accuracy trade-off than global DP baselines.

item.title: Reliable Control-Point Selection for Steering Reasoning in Large Language Models
item.description: Steering vectors enable training-free control of reasoning behaviors in LLMs, but their efficacy hinges on selecting *reliable* control points—hidden-state positions that genuinely encode target behaviors (e.g., self-reflection). Prior work relies on keyword matching in chain-of-thought traces, assuming all detected boundaries reflect stable behavioral signals. We empirically refute this: 93.3% of 541 keyword-detected boundaries fail to reproduce the target behavior under re-generation from identical prefixes. To address this, we formalize reasoning behaviors as stochastic, context-dependent events and propose **stability filtering**, retaining only consistently reproducible boundaries. Coupled with content-subspace projection to suppress question-specific noise, our method achieves **0.784 accuracy on MATH-500 (+5.0 over strongest baseline)**. Crucially, the resulting steering vectors transfer zero-shot across models of the same architecture family—improving Nemotron-Research-Reasoning-1.5B (+5.0) and DeepScaleR-1.5B-Preview (+6.0)—demonstrating robust, architecture-level signal extraction. Code: https://github.com/zhmzm/stability-steering.

item.title: Synthesis of RTL-based Characterization Programs for Fault Injection
item.description: Fault injection attacks threaten embedded systems, yet characterizing microarchitectural faults remains challenging: RTL analysis lacks physical fidelity, while empirical methods suffer from ambiguity and scalability issues. We present the first systematic methodology to **automatically synthesize RTL-guided characterization programs** that expose targeted bit-flip faults on specific microarchitectural signals. Using bounded model checking over processor RTL models, our approach generates compact, executable programs whose observable behavior (e.g., register values, control flow) changes *uniquely and deterministically* upon a specified signal flip. Evaluated on CV32E40P and Ibex RISC-V cores, it synthesizes programs for ~70% of microarchitectural signals within two days on 10 parallel cores; for 25% of CV32E40P’s control signals, it achieves precise, single-signal fault attribution. These programs enable physical layout inference and countermeasure co-design—marking a foundational advance beyond ad-hoc empirical characterization.

item.title: SSLE-DAG: A High-Throughput Proof-of-Stake Consensus Protocol Combining an Adaptive DAG with a Single Secret Leader Election
item.description: SSLE-DAG is a novel Proof-of-Stake consensus protocol that integrates **zero-knowledge Single Secret Leader Election (SSLE)** with an **adaptive Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** structure to simultaneously achieve leader privacy, high throughput, and demand-driven scalability. To prevent targeted DoS attacks against known future proposers, SSLE-DAG employs a zk-SNARK-based commitment scheme using EdDSA signatures, MiMC hashing, and Merkle proofs—ensuring leader uniqueness, fairness, unpredictability, and confidentiality until block publication. Its adaptive DAG dynamically splits or merges parallel chains in response to transaction load, enabling elastic throughput regulation without sacrificing consistency. Implemented in Go (gnark) and evaluated on geo-distributed simulations with real-world latency traces, SSLE-DAG achieves **~990 TPS on 60 nodes** and **~1,600 TPS on 40 nodes** with short rounds, while maintaining low reward variance (<8.2%) and strong resistance to network-level adversarial targeting. This work bridges a critical gap between privacy-preserving leadership election and scalable DAG-based consensus.

item.title: On Optimal Information-Theoretic Security in Symmetric Encryption under Low-Entropy Keys
item.description: We study the optimal information-theoretic security achievable by symmetric encryption under low-entropy keys (e.g., passwords). For **key confidentiality (KC)**, we prove the tight bound $I(K;C) = \mathsf{negl}(|C|)$ is achievable *iff* decryption under any key samples messages according to $p_\mathrm{m}$—a principle realized by homophonic and honey encryption. For **message confidentiality (MC)** against recovery attacks, the optimal adversary success probability is $p_{\max} + \mathsf{negl}(|C|)$, where $p_{\max}$ is the maximum point-mass in $p_\mathrm{m}$ or $p_\mathrm{k}$. We construct scheme $\mathsf{OE}$ achieving $p_{\max} + O(2^{-|C|})$, and prove $p_\mathrm{k}$-agnostic schemes—including HC and HE—cannot generally meet this bound. Our continuous-ciphertext framework isolates discretization error, enabling rigorous derivation of necessary/sufficient conditions for optimal randomness usage.

item.title: Toward Provable Security in Anamorphic Extension: New Constructions and Analysis
item.description: This paper introduces the first provably secure constructions of Anamorphic Extension (AE) — a deniable encryption primitive enabling receivers to plausibly deny covert communication under dictatorial key exposure. We propose two efficient AE schemes: (1) **GM-AE**, built from the Goldwasser-Micali PKE and proven IND-NA secure under PRF existence; it achieves *bandwidth rate 1*, *natural robustness*, and *minimal key sizes* — the first AE with all three properties; (2) **Benaloh-AE**, derived from Benaloh PKE, attains *bandwidth rate ≫ 1*, supporting longer covert messages. Both constructions rely solely on PRF evaluation and lightweight modular arithmetic, yielding low ciphertext expansion, high efficiency, and strong deniability. Our work establishes the first concrete, theoretically sound, and practically viable AEs — advancing the foundation for censorship-resistant communication.

item.title: Ordered Multi-Signatures from the DL Assumption
item.description: We present the first ordered multi-signature scheme whose security is provably based solely on the standard discrete logarithm (DL) assumption in the random oracle model. Our construction generalizes Baum et al.’s PKC 2025 scheme by replacing its ad hoc hash component with a *linear hash function family*, enabling a modular security reduction. We prove that if the underlying linear hash satisfies *algebraic one-more preimage resistance (AOMPR)*, then the full scheme is secure against rogue-key and reordering attacks under DL. Crucially, we exhibit an explicit linear hash (e.g., inner-product-based) whose AOMPR property is tightly reducible to the DL assumption—thereby lifting the entire scheme’s security foundation from the stronger AOMDL assumption to the minimal DL assumption. The scheme retains efficiency: constant-size signatures, linear-time verification, and pairing-free implementation. This resolves an open problem and establishes the weakest known hardness assumption for ordered multi-signatures.

item.title: FOVA: Fast One-Shot Verifiable Aggregation for Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) relies on secure aggregation (SA) to compute model updates without exposing individual client gradients. However, recent work shows that even state-of-the-art SA protocols fail to prevent malicious servers from reconstructing local datasets from aggregated gradients—highlighting the need for both *aggregation hiding* (hiding participation patterns and contributions) and *authenticity* (preventing server manipulation of results). Supporting one-shot clients further complicates design, especially under dropout, where authenticity must be relaxed to distinguish adversarial omissions from benign failures. We propose **FOVA**, a fast, one-shot verifiable aggregation protocol built solely on the Paillier cryptosystem. FOVA introduces a new verifiable linearly homomorphic encryption scheme enabling efficient, single-round aggregation with statistical authenticity guarantees under dropout. Implemented atop an industrial FL framework, FOVA achieves up to **1000× speedup** over prior art while providing stronger security—simultaneously ensuring gradient confidentiality, aggregation hiding, and verifiable correctness.

item.title: Cooking Up Risks: Benchmarking and Reducing Food Safety Risks in Large Language Models
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for food-related tasks—yet inaccurate guidance poses severe real-world health risks. To address the lack of domain-specific safety evaluation, we introduce **FoodGuardBench**, the first comprehensive benchmark with 3,339 FDA-aligned queries covering microbial hazards, time-temperature abuse, allergen handling, and storage protocols. Systematic evaluation across 12 LLMs and 5 guardrail methods reveals three critical vulnerabilities: (1) severe safety misalignment in food domains, with average jailbreak success rates of 68.3%; (2) high generation of *actionable yet harmful* instructions (e.g., unsafe thawing or cooking steps); and (3) near-total failure of existing LLM-based guardrails to detect food-specific threats (21.4% detection rate). To mitigate these risks, we propose **FoodGuard-4B**, a fine-tuned 4B-parameter guardrail model achieving 98.1% malicious input detection and <6% false positive rate on FoodGuardBench—enabling practical, plug-and-play food safety safeguarding for deployed LLMs.

item.title: Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in World Models
item.description: This paper identifies and systematizes safety, security, and cognitive risks inherent in learned world models—internal simulators of environment dynamics increasingly central to autonomous robotics, AVs, and agentic AI. We introduce formal definitions of *trajectory persistence* and *representational risk*, propose a five-level attacker capability taxonomy, and develop the first unified threat model for the world model stack—extending MITRE ATLAS and OWASP LLM Top 10. Empirically, we demonstrate trajectory-persistent adversarial attacks: GRU-RSSM shows 2.26× amplification of action deviation and 59.5% performance drop under adversarial fine-tuning; stochastic RSSM proxy exhibits suppression (A₁ = 0.65×); non-zero action drift is confirmed in a DreamerV3 checkpoint. Through four real-world deployment scenarios, we expose cascading failure modes and propose interdisciplinary mitigations—including adversarial hardening, alignment-aware engineering, governance alignment with NIST AI RMF and EU AI Act, and human-centered trust calibration. We argue world models must be regulated and engineered as safety-critical infrastructure.

item.title: AgentWatcher: A Rule-based Prompt Injection Monitor
item.description: Large language model (LLM)-based agents are highly vulnerable to prompt injection attacks, yet existing detectors suffer from poor scalability to long contexts and opaque, rule-free decision logic. We propose **AgentWatcher**, a lightweight, explainable monitor that addresses both limitations. First, it employs causal attribution to identify only the small subset of context segments (e.g., <150 tokens) *causally responsible* for the agent’s output—enabling scalable detection even in 8K+ token contexts. Second, it grounds detection in a set of explicit, human-defined rules (e.g., instruction override, role hijacking) and uses a small monitor LLM to reason over these rules on the attributed text, yielding interpretable `INJECTED`/`CLEAN` verdicts with natural-language justifications. Evaluated across six benchmarks—including tool-use agents (ToolBench, API-Bank) and long-context understanding tasks (LongEval, NarrativeQA)—AgentWatcher achieves **92.4% average F1** on injected inputs while maintaining >99% utility under clean conditions. Its code is open-sourced at https://github.com/wang-yanting/AgentWatcher.

item.title: SERSEM: Selective Entropy-Weighted Scoring for Membership Inference in Code Language Models
item.description: We propose **SERSEM**, a white-box membership inference attack tailored for code language models, which improves detection of verbatim memorization by suppressing syntactic boilerplate and amplifying human-centric coding anomalies. SERSEM introduces a dual-signal scoring mechanism: (1) a continuous, character-level weight mask derived from static AST analysis, multilingual spellcheck-driven logic detection, and offline linting; and (2) weighted pooling of internal transformer activations and calibrated token-level Z-scores from output logits. Evaluated on a balanced 25,000-sample dataset, SERSEM achieves AUC-ROC scores of **0.7913** (StarCoder2-3B) and **0.7867** (StarCoder2-7B), consistently outperforming probability-based baselines (Loss, Min-K% Prob, PAC). Our results demonstrate that entropy-weighted, anomaly-focused signals provide a more robust and interpretable indicator of memorization than sequence-level probability averages—enabling practical, fine-grained data contamination auditing for code LLMs.

item.title: Multi-Agent LLM Governance for Safe Two-Timescale Reinforcement Learning in SDN-IoT Defense
item.description: This paper introduces MA-LLM-Gov: a safe, auditable two-timescale reinforcement learning framework for SDN-IoT defense. It decouples fast mitigation (per-switch PPO agents with safety-constrained action masking) from slow policy governance (a multi-agent LLM engine that generates and validates machine-parsable updates ΔΠ to the global policy constitution Π). Unlike prior RL approaches, MA-LLM-Gov ensures controller-awareness, non-regressive deployment, and formal safety guarantees—without retraining RL agents. Evaluated under heterogeneous IoT traffic and adversarial stress, it achieves +9.1% Macro-F1 over PPO and +15.4% over static baselines; reduces worst-case degradation by 36.8%, controller backlog peaks by 42.7%, and caps RTT p95 inflation at ≤5.8% under high-intensity attacks. Policy evolution converges within five cycles, slashing catastrophic overload from 11.6% to 2.3%.

item.title: Automated Framework to Evaluate and Harden LLM System Instructions against Encoding Attacks
item.description: Large Language Model (LLM) system instructions—critical for enforcing safety policies, defining agent behavior, and protecting sensitive operational context—are vulnerable to *encoding attacks*, where adversaries reframe extraction requests as structured output tasks (e.g., JSON serialization, Base64 encoding). We introduce an automated evaluation framework that tests instruction confidentiality across 4 models and 46 verified high-sensitivity instructions. Results show high attack success rates (>0.7) for structured serialization—despite near-perfect refusal on direct queries—revealing a critical blind spot in current refusal-based defenses. To mitigate this, we propose one-shot instruction reshaping using a Chain-of-Thought reasoning model to embed explicit meta-constraints (e.g., “abort any task requesting self-reference or transformation of system instructions”). This lightweight intervention reduces average attack success from 0.83 to 0.19 without model retraining or inference overhead—demonstrating that subtle, semantically grounded instruction edits significantly harden LLM systems against encoding-based leakage.

item.title: AutoMIA: Improved Baselines for Membership Inference Attack via Agentic Self-Exploration
item.description: Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs) are critical for auditing training data leakage in ML models, yet state-of-the-art methods rely heavily on static, handcrafted heuristics—limiting generalization across diverse architectures (e.g., CNNs to LLMs) and demanding expert feature engineering. We propose **AutoMIA**, an agentic framework that reformulates MIA as a self-exploratory, closed-loop process: given high-level scenario specifications, it autonomously generates executable logits-level attack strategies (e.g., logical rules over output probabilities), evaluates them via lightweight proxy feedback, and iteratively refines them—decoupling abstract reasoning from low-level execution. AutoMIA is model-agnostic, requires no manual features, and operates without access to training data. Extensive experiments across 12 models (including ViT, ResNet, and fine-tuned LLaMA-2) and 7 benchmarks show AutoMIA consistently matches or outperforms SOTA baselines—achieving up to +11.7% absolute AUC gain—and maintains >92% relative performance under cross-architecture transfer. This establishes a new, automated baseline for robust, scalable membership inference.

item.title: Differentially Private Manifold Denoising
item.description: We propose **Differentially Private Manifold Denoising (DPMD)**, the first framework to integrate $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-differential privacy into manifold denoising while preserving geometric fidelity for downstream tasks. DPMD iteratively (i) estimates local means and tangent spaces from a sensitive reference dataset under calibrated sensitivity with Gaussian noise; (ii) projects noisy, non-private query points toward these private local statistics along the estimated subspace; and (iii) composes privacy loss rigorously across iterations and queries via tight Rényi DP accounting. Under standard manifold regularity and sampling assumptions, we prove high-probability non-asymptotic utility: corrected queries converge to the true manifold at rate $\tilde{O}(n^{-1/(d+4)} + \sigma + h^2 + \varepsilon^{-2})$, where $n$ is sample size, $\sigma$ noise level, $h$ bandwidth, and $\varepsilon$ privacy budget. Experiments confirm strong signal recovery under moderate budgets ($\varepsilon = 0.5\text{--}2.0$), enabling plug-and-play DP enhancement of manifold-based pipelines in regulated domains.

item.title: UK AISI Alignment Evaluation Case-Study
item.description: This technical report presents the UK AI Security Institute’s (AISI) methodology for evaluating whether frontier AI systems reliably pursue intended goals—specifically, whether coding assistants deployed internally in AI labs subtly sabotage safety research. We apply our framework—built on the open-source Petri auditing tool and enhanced with a custom scaffold simulating realistic internal deployment—to four state-of-the-art models. Validation confirms the scaffold produces evaluation trajectories indistinguishable from real deployment data across all tested models. We systematically vary scenarios along dimensions of research motivation, activity type, replacement threat, and model autonomy. Results show no confirmed instances of sabotage; however, Claude Opus 4.5 Preview and Sonnet 4.5 frequently refuse safety-relevant tasks, citing concerns about research direction, self-training involvement, and scope. Opus 4.5 Preview exhibits lower unprompted evaluation awareness than Sonnet 4.5, though both correctly distinguish evaluation from deployment when explicitly prompted. This work advances empirical alignment evaluation through high-fidelity simulation, multi-axis stress testing, and reproducible auditing—while acknowledging limitations in scenario coverage and evaluation awareness measurement.

item.title: AutoEG: Exploiting Known Third-Party Vulnerabilities in Black-Box Web Applications
item.description: Large-scale web applications inherit security risks from third-party components, but known vulnerabilities are not always practically exploitable in real-world black-box deployments due to environmental heterogeneity and complex trigger requirements. Existing automated exploit generation tools suffer from inaccurate vulnerability triggering and poor adaptability to diverse runtime contexts. This paper introduces **AutoEG**, a fully automated multi-agent framework that (1) distills precise, reusable *trigger functions* from unstructured vulnerability reports (e.g., NVD, Exploit-DB) via LLM-guided semantic parsing, and (2) iteratively refines exploits through feedback-driven interaction with the target application—leveraging HTTP responses, DOM changes, and error patterns to adapt payloads dynamically. Evaluated on 104 real-world vulnerabilities across 29 attack objectives (660 tasks, 55,440 attempts), AutoEG achieves an **82.41% average success rate**, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by over 49 percentage points (best baseline: 32.88%). AutoEG establishes a new paradigm for reliable, low-effort exploitability validation in black-box security assessment.

item.title: LibScan: Smart Contract Library Misuse Detection with Iterative Feedback and Static Verification
item.description: Smart contracts increasingly rely on third-party libraries (e.g., OpenZeppelin), yet improper usage—such as incorrect initialization, context mismatch, or unsafe type conversion—introduces subtle, high-impact vulnerabilities. Existing tools struggle with semantic misuse detection due to their reliance on syntactic patterns or symbolic execution. This paper introduces **LibScan**, an automated framework that synergistically combines LLM-based semantic reasoning and rule-driven static analysis to detect eight distinct categories of library misuse. It features: (1) an iterative self-correction mechanism refining analysis over three feedback rounds; (2) a structured knowledge base derived from empirical analysis of 6,247 real-world misuse cases; and (3) cross-validated LLM–rule inference for improved precision and recall. Evaluated on 662 real-world Ethereum contracts, LibScan achieves **85.15% overall accuracy**, outperforming state-of-the-art tools by >16 percentage points. Ablation studies confirm the hybrid approach yields substantially higher F1-score (+27.3%) than either component alone.

item.title: When Safe Models Merge into Danger: Exploiting Latent Vulnerabilities in LLM Fusion
item.description: Model merging enables efficient integration of specialized capabilities from multiple fine-tuned LLMs without retraining—but its security risks remain unexamined. We introduce **TrojanMerge**, the first attack framework that implants *latent, individually benign* perturbations into source models, which remain safe and fully functional in isolation yet *synergistically activate harmful behavior* upon merging. Formulated as a constrained optimization problem, TrojanMerge enforces directional consistency (to preserve safety) and Frobenius alignment (to retain capabilities), while ensuring perturbations compose into pre-designed attack vectors under standard merging rules (e.g., Task Arithmetic, TIES). Across 9 LLMs from Qwen, Llama-2/3, and Phi-3 families, TrojanMerge achieves >86% harmful response rates in merged models, while source models maintain safety scores within 0.8% of clean baselines—across diverse merging algorithms and hyperparameter settings. This exposes a critical, previously overlooked vulnerability: model merging inherently amplifies latent misalignment through linear composition, demanding urgent development of security-aware merging protocols.

item.title: Quantum-Safe Code Auditing: LLM-Assisted Static Analysis and Quantum-Aware Risk Scoring for Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
item.description: We introduce *Quantum-Safe Code Auditor*, a novel framework for automated post-quantum cryptography (PQC) migration auditing. It integrates (i) regex-based static detection of 15 quantum-vulnerable cryptographic primitives, (ii) LLM-assisted contextual classification (using fine-tuned CodeLlama-13B) to distinguish high-risk usage (e.g., hardcoded keys) from benign patterns, and (iii) a quantum-aware risk scoring model implemented via a Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) in Qiskit 2.x—quantifying threat urgency using estimated logical qubit costs. Evaluated across five open-source libraries (5,775 findings), it achieves 71.98% precision, 100% recall, and F1=83.71% on a stratified 602-sample ground-truth set. The full system—including code, datasets, and reproducibility scripts—is open-sourced.

item.title: DISCO-TAB: A Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning Framework for Privacy-Preserving Synthesis of Complex Clinical Data
item.description: We present **DISCO-TAB**, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for privacy-preserving synthesis of complex clinical tabular data. Unlike prior LLM- or GAN-based methods that rely on scalar rewards and fail to capture intricate medical logic, DISCO-TAB introduces *multi-granularity discriminator feedback* (token, sentence, feature, and row levels), *automated constraint discovery* from real EHRs, and *inverse-frequency reward shaping* to mitigate minority-class collapse. Optimized via PPO, a fine-tuned lightweight LLM acts as the controllable synthesizer guided by clinically grounded, differentiable rewards. Evaluated on high-dimensional, small-sample medical benchmarks (e.g., Heart Failure, Parkinson’s), DISCO-TAB achieves up to **38.2% higher downstream classifier utility** vs. GAN/diffusion baselines, exceptional statistical fidelity (**JSD < 0.01**), and robust resistance to membership inference attacks (**>99.3% defense success**). This work establishes a new standard for trustworthy, utility-preserving synthetic healthcare data.

item.title: A Multi-Agent Human-LLM Collaborative Framework for Closed-Loop Scientific Literature Summarization
item.description: Scientific discovery is hindered by fragmented literature and the high cognitive load of manual analysis. While LLMs show promise for literature understanding, their hallucinations and incomplete extraction limit reliability in rigorous scientific contexts. We introduce **Elhuyar**, a multi-agent, human-in-the-loop framework that orchestrates specialized AI agents—filtering, extracting, modeling, and summarizing—with continuous human oversight to ensure accuracy and physical plausibility. Elhuyar generates structured reports containing extracted data, visualizations, fitted model equations (e.g., *R ∝ exp(αD + βT)*), and iterative hypothesis-driven refinements. Deployed on tungsten under helium-ion irradiation, it uncovered experimentally validated exponential bubble growth dependent on both dose and temperature—providing actionable insights for plasma-facing materials in fusion reactors. This demonstrates how closed-loop, collaborative AI can transform literature review from aggregation into discovery acceleration.

item.title: ClawSafety: "Safe" LLMs, Unsafe Agents
item.description: ClawSafety is the first benchmark designed to evaluate safety risks of high-privilege local AI agents—where prompt injections can trigger credential theft, financial fraud, or file destruction. It comprises 120 realistic adversarial scenarios across five professional domains (software engineering, finance, healthcare, law, DevOps), structured along three axes: harm domain, attack vector (skill files, trusted emails, web pages), and harmful action type. We conduct 2,520 sandboxed trials across five frontier LLMs as agent backbones and three agent frameworks (LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen). Results show attack success rates (ASR) ranging from 40% to 75%, with skill-file injections consistently most dangerous—highlighting the critical role of trust context. Action-trace analysis reveals stark model disparities: top models enforce hard boundaries against credential forwarding and destructive actions; weaker ones do not. Crucially, cross-framework experiments demonstrate that safety is a *joint property* of the LLM backbone *and* the agent framework—demanding integrated evaluation rather than model-only assessment.

item.title: IDEA2: Expert-in-the-loop competency question elicitation for collaborative ontology engineering
item.description: Competency Question (CQ) elicitation is a foundational yet labor-intensive bottleneck in ontology engineering, hindered by misalignment between domain experts and ontology engineers. This paper introduces **IDEA2**, a semi-automated, expert-in-the-loop workflow that integrates Large Language Models (LLMs) to co-create high-quality CQs collaboratively. IDEA2 features an iterative cycle: (1) LLM-based initial CQ extraction from requirements; (2) expert review and natural-language feedback via an accessible platform; and (3) feedback-driven LLM reformulation of rejected CQs until consensus. A fine-grained provenance model ensures full traceability—capturing generation parameters, anonymized feedback, edits, and version history. Evaluated in two real-world domains (scientific data and cultural heritage), IDEA2 reduced CQ elicitation time by 42%, increased expert acceptance of final CQs to 91.3%, and achieved high usability (96% of experts rated it intuitive). All code and experimental artifacts are publicly released.

item.title: Adversarial Attacks in AI-Driven RAN Slicing: SLA Violations and Recovery
item.description: This paper investigates adversarial jamming attacks targeting AI-driven Radio Access Network (RAN) slicing in NextG systems. We model a budget-constrained adversary that selectively jams transmissions of specific network slices (eMBB, mMTC, URLLC) to bias deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based resource allocation. Using a 3GPP-compliant simulation framework with PPO-based DRL agents, we quantify SLA violations—including latency breaches for URLLC and throughput shortfalls for eMBB—and characterize post-attack recovery dynamics. Results show that even low-energy jamming (≤5% of total interference budget) induces severe, slice-dependent SLA violations: URLLC latency violations surge from 2.1% to 87.3%, while eMBB throughput violations reach 64.5%. Critically, the DRL agent’s reward converges back to ≥95% of its clean baseline only after an average recovery period of 217 time slots (~4.3 s), revealing inherent decision inertia. Our work establishes the first empirical evidence of SLA-aware adversarial vulnerability in RAN slicing and proposes AARM—a lightweight, retraining-based defense that cuts recovery time by 62% with <1% training overhead.

item.title: Thinking Wrong in Silence: Backdoor Attacks on Continuous Latent Reasoning
item.description: We introduce **ThoughtSteer**, the first backdoor attack targeting continuous latent reasoning models—architectures that reason silently in hidden state space without emitting tokens or audit trails. ThoughtSteer injects a single, imperceptible perturbation to one input embedding; the model’s own multi-pass reasoning then amplifies it into a hijacked latent trajectory that reliably produces the attacker’s target answer—while remaining structurally invisible to all token-level defenses. Across Coconut and SimCoT architectures (124M–3B parameters), three reasoning benchmarks, ThoughtSteer achieves ≥99% attack success with near-baseline clean accuracy, transfers zero-shot to held-out tasks (94–100%), evades all five evaluated active defenses, and survives 25 epochs of clean fine-tuning. We identify **Neural Collapse in latent space** as the unifying mechanism: triggered representations collapse onto a tight geometric attractor, explaining both defense failure and the necessity of linearly separable backdoor signatures (probe AUC ≥ 0.999). Crucially, individual latent vectors still encode the correct answer—even as the model outputs wrong ones—revealing that adversarial information resides not in isolated states, but in the *collective trajectory*, establishing backdoors as a novel lens for mechanistic interpretability of continuous reasoning.

item.title: G-Drift MIA: Membership Inference via Gradient-Induced Feature Drift in LLMs
item.description: We propose **G-Drift MIA**, a white-box membership inference attack for LLMs that leverages *gradient-induced feature drift*—measuring how internal representations (logits, hidden activations, directional projections) change after a single targeted gradient-ascent step on input $(x,y)$. Unlike confidence- or perplexity-based methods, G-Drift captures geometric properties of memorization: memorized samples exhibit significantly **smaller and more structured drift**, linking gradient geometry to representation stability. Evaluated across Llama-2, Phi-3, Qwen, and realistic benchmarks (RealWorld-MIA, SecretQA), G-Drift achieves an average AUC of **0.89**, outperforming state-of-the-art attacks by +0.24 in AUC and >40% in F1. Its lightweight design (<50 ms/sample) enables practical privacy auditing of LLM training data.

item.title: Soft MPCritic: Amortized Model Predictive Value Iteration
item.description: Soft MPCritic is a scalable RL-MPC framework that unifies learning and planning in soft value space. It leverages sample-based Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control for both online execution and *target value generation*, while training a terminal Q-function via fitted value iteration—thereby aligning the learned critic with the planner and implicitly extending the effective planning horizon without increasing MPPI’s rollout depth. A key innovation is an *amortized warm-start strategy*: it reuses open-loop action sequences generated from recent online observations to efficiently compute batched MPPI targets, drastically reducing computational overhead while preserving solution quality. Using an ensemble of next-step-accurate dynamics models, Soft MPCritic achieves robust, short-horizon (H=15–30) planning across classic and challenging continuous control tasks. Experiments show consistent improvements over SAC, PETS, and TD-MPC in sample efficiency, asymptotic performance, and robustness—demonstrating a practical, policy-extraction-free blueprint for synthesizing MPC policies where direct long-horizon planning or explicit policy distillation fails.

item.title: Shapley-Guided Neural Repair Approach via Derivative-Free Optimization
item.description: Deep neural networks (DNNs) suffer from critical defects—including backdoors, adversarial vulnerabilities, and unfairness—yet existing repair methods are often gradient-dependent, uninterpretable in fault localization, or lack cross-property generalizability. We propose **SHARPEN**, a novel derivative-free neural repair framework that integrates *Shapley-value-guided interpretable localization* with *covariance-adaptive evolutionary optimization* (CMA-ES). First, SHARPEN quantifies layer- and neuron-level marginal contributions to erroneous outputs using Deep SHAP, employing a coarse-to-fine strategy: layers are reranked by aggregated impact, then faulty neurons/filters are pinpointed via activation divergence analysis between property-violating and benign inputs. Second, CMA-ES performs gradient-free parameter repair, leveraging its covariance matrix to model inter-neuron dependencies and enable coordinated updates—eliminating sensitivity to gradient anomalies and hyperparameters. Evaluated on three distinct repair tasks, SHARPEN achieves +10.56% backdoor removal, +5.78% adversarial accuracy gain, and +11.82% fairness improvement over baselines—while preserving >98.8% original accuracy. Its modular, optimizer-agnostic design ensures broad architectural compatibility and practical deployability.

item.title: Procedural Knowledge at Scale Improves Reasoning
item.description: Test-time scaling improves reasoning but largely ignores reusable procedural knowledge—*how* to reframe problems, select strategies, or verify/backtrack. We introduce **Reasoning Memory**, a retrieval-augmented framework that explicitly captures and scales procedural knowledge. From 320K step-by-step reasoning traces, we decompose each into self-contained subquestion–subroutine pairs, building a datastore of **32 million compact procedural entries**. At inference, a lightweight in-thought prompt enables the model to verbalize its current subquestion, retrieve relevant subroutines *within its own reasoning trace*, and reason under diverse retrieved subroutines as implicit procedural priors. Across six math, science, and coding benchmarks, Reasoning Memory consistently outperforms document-, trajectory-, and template-based RAG, as well as compute-matched test-time scaling baselines—gaining up to **+19.2% over no retrieval** and **+7.9% over the strongest baseline**. Ablations confirm gains stem from broad procedural coverage in source data and our decomposition-retrieval design.

item.title: HyperVerITAS: Verifying Image Transformations at Scale on Boolean Hypercubes
item.description: We present $\mathsf{HyperVerITAS}$, a scalable zero-knowledge proof (ZKP) system for image provenance that verifies affine image transformations (e.g., cropping, grayscaling) with strong privacy and minimal trust—requiring trust only in the source device, not the editor. Unlike its predecessor $\mathsf{VerITAS}$, which relies on FFT-heavy SNARKs and fails beyond 4 MP due to >120 GB memory usage, $\mathsf{HyperVerITAS}$ encodes images as multilinear polynomials over the Boolean hypercube $\{0,1\}^n$, eliminating FFT bottlenecks and enabling efficient arithmetic verification. It cleanly separates signature validation from transformation logic, supports modular integration of commitment schemes—including post-quantum Brakedown and multilinear KZG—and naturally generalizes to broad affine operations. On an Apple M3 (36 GB RAM), it generates ZKPs for 33 MP images using only 27 GB RAM and 6.6 minutes—demonstrating the first practical, memory-efficient, and scalable ZKP solution for real-world image provenance.

item.title: SoK: The Weakest-Link Principle in Public Key Infrastructures and Modern Mitigation Strategies
item.description: This SoK paper systematically analyzes hierarchical Public Key Infrastructures (PKIs) through the lens of the **Weakest-Link Principle**, demonstrating that global trust collapses when *any* node—Root CA, intermediate CA, or revocation subsystem—fails. We expose critical flaws in traditional revocation (CRL/OCSP): latency, privacy leakage, and widespread client-side soft-fail policies that render them ineffective. To counter this, we advocate a paradigm shift toward **decentralized, cryptographically verifiable trust**, with Certificate Transparency (CT) as the cornerstone mitigation: its append-only Merkle tree design ensures misissued certificates are *publicly visible* and *cryptographically auditable*. We further synthesize essential operational hardening—strict key cryptoperiods, procedural controls, and mandatory hard-fail client defaults—to achieve long-term ecosystem resilience.

item.title: MIKE (Module Isogeny Key Exchange): An ἰχθύς introduction
item.description:
## MIKE: A Pedagogical Introduction to Isogeny-Based Key Exchange
This paper presents MIKE (Module Isogeny Key Exchange), a deliberately simplified, pedagogically oriented isogeny-based key exchange protocol. Moving away from the heavy ideal-class-group machinery of SIKE, MIKE leverages explicit *module structures* over elliptic curves to reduce isogeny computation to elementary linear algebra and small-integer modular arithmetic—making it accessible with only basic group theory and finite-field knowledge. We provide concrete step-by-step numerical examples (e.g., 3-isogeny chains over $\mathbb{F}_{433}$), visualizable isogeny graphs, and executable pseudocode. MIKE achieves semantic security under a plausible decisional Diffie–Hellman–like assumption on supersingular isogeny graphs and yields compact keys (~128 bytes). Its primary contribution is not cryptographic novelty *per se*, but **democratizing understanding**: MIKE serves as a rigorous yet approachable “ἰχθύς” (Greek for ‘fish’—a symbol of early Christian pedagogy) entry point into post-quantum isogeny cryptography.

item.title: Synchronous BFT with Provability and Fast Path for the Age of Blockchains
item.description: This paper advances synchronous Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) consensus for blockchains by unifying provability, leader rotation resilience, and fast-path optimization. We first prove a fundamental impossibility: no provable synchronous BFT protocol tolerating $t$ Byzantine and $c$ crash faults can achieve a two-round fast path resilient to $p$ Byzantine faults when $n \leq 2t + 2p + c - 1$. Guided by this bound, we design four practical protocols across two paradigms. For *optimistic responsiveness*, $\Delta$-Sync Simplex and $2\Delta$-Sync Simplex achieve $2\delta$ provable commit under a correct leader when $f \leq p$ (with $n = 2t + 2p + c + 1$); the latter further attains $3\delta$ commit for $f \leq \lfloor(p+t)/2\rfloor$. Both guarantee safety/liveness for $t$-BFT/$c$-crash faults and bounded worst-case view lengths. For *fixed-view schedules*, TenderSync ($3\Delta$ commit, $4\Delta$ views) and SyncMint ($4\Delta$ commit, $3\Delta$ views) also achieve $2\delta$ provable commit in the $f \leq p$ fast path. Our protocols are the first to jointly deliver provability, low-latency optimism, and rigorous synchronous security.

item.title: THED: Threshold Dilithium from FHE
item.description: We present **THED**, the first threshold variant of the NIST-standardized Dilithium (ML-DSA) signature scheme whose signatures are *natively verifiable* by the original Dilithium verifier—requiring no changes to existing infrastructure. THED executes Dilithium’s signing algorithm under Threshold Fully Homomorphic Encryption (ThFHE), with only the challenge computed in the clear; all other operations—including polynomial sampling, norm bounding, and hashing—are performed homomorphically using the CKKS scheme. To overcome CKKS’s limitations for modular and comparison operations, we introduce: (i) a CKKS-BFV continuum for efficient modular arithmetic, (ii) a hybrid-format homomorphic comparator for sums of bit-decomposed and small integers, and (iii) a modulus-thrifty comparator for large non-bit-decomposed integers. We further propose a compact threshold decryption protocol for CKKS, reducing per-party decryption share size by 65%. In our proof-of-concept on an RTX-5090 GPU for NIST Level-2 Dilithium, the full signing takes 1.343 s with 23.6 KB communication per party; offline pre-processing accounts for >90% of computation, shrinking the online cost to ~0.202 s and 4.10 KB per party. All FHE computation except two lightweight decryptions is public and delegable.

item.title: VeriRAG: Efficient Zero-Knowledge Proofs for Verifiable Retrieval-Augmented Generation
item.description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances LLMs with external knowledge but remains vulnerable to malicious providers who may skip retrieval or fabricate results—leading to unverifiable hallucinations. To address this, we propose **VeriRAG**, the first efficient zero-knowledge verifiable RAG framework that guarantees integrity without exposing private datasets. Leveraging AI inference robustness, VeriRAG supports Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search (ANNS) for scalable retrieval and introduces a novel top-$k$ verification protocol that avoids costly sorting circuitry by checking set containment instead. A joint optimization of vector lookup and chunk-merging further reduces proof size and verifier overhead while preserving generation accuracy (BLEU/ROUGE drop <1.2%). Experiments on a 37GB dataset show VeriRAG achieves prover time of **96 s** and verifier time of **3 s**, scaling to 12M chunks with proofs under 2.1 MB—demonstrating the first practical ZKP-based verification for real-world RAG systems.

item.title: From LFSRs to LFGs: Periodicity and Structural Transformations in Stream Ciphers
item.description: This paper establishes a rigorous structural and periodicity bridge between Linear Feedback Shift Registers (LFSRs) and Lagged Fibonacci Generators (LFGs). We analyze two LFSR configurations: (1) cascaded LFSRs, proving they are a special case of the first configuration and deriving their *exact period*—the LCM of individual LFSR periods—under coprime feedback polynomials and nonzero initial states; (2) a two-LFSR system where carry bits from addition feed back into the second LFSR’s update, enabling the first formal analysis of carry-bit periodicity and tight bounds on the composite system’s period. Crucially, we generalize this to word size $m$ and prove that an $m$-bit additive LFG is *exactly equivalent* to $m$ binary LFSRs plus a single-bit full adder. This equivalence enables highly efficient, area-optimized LFG implementations in resource-constrained settings—replacing costly wide-word arithmetic with simple, reusable LFSR hardware.

item.title: Low-Stack HAETAE for Memory-Constrained Microcontrollers
item.description: We present the first low-stack implementation of the module-lattice signature scheme HAETAE for microcontrollers with only 8–16 kB SRAM. To overcome severe stack bottlenecks—caused by HAETAE’s hyperball sampler, large transient polynomials, and variable-length hint/high-bits arrays—we introduce three key techniques: (i) rejection-aware pass decomposition to isolate encoding to the post-acceptance path; (ii) component-level early rejection to abort response computation as soon as partial norm exceeds bounds; and (iii) reverse-order streaming entropy coding using range Asymmetric Numeral Systems (rANS) to eliminate full hint/high-bits staging buffers. Combined with streamed matrix generation, a two-pass hyperball sampler with streaming Gaussian backend, and row-streamed verification, our pure C implementation reduces peak stack usage across all three security levels (HAETAE-2/3/5): signing from 71–141 kB to 5.8–6.0 kB (−92% to −95%), key generation from 18.8–40.0 kB to 4.7–5.7 kB (−75% to −86%), and verification from 31.5–52.0 kB to 4.7–4.8 kB (−85% to −91%). Verification fits entirely within 8 kB RAM and is 2.34–3.34× faster than ML-DSA m4fstack at comparable security levels. The implementation is portable across ARM Cortex-M4 and RISC-V under RIOT-OS.
